primary_diagnosis.name: Subacute (de Quervain's) thyroiditis with transient thyrotoxicosis

pathophysiology_summary: A post-viral granulomatous inflammation of the thyroid gland, typically following an upper-respiratory viral illness by 2–6 weeks. Inflammatory destruction of thyroid follicles causes exquisite anterior neck pain (classically radiating to the jaw and ears) and unregulated release of pre-formed stored thyroid hormone, producing a transient thyrotoxic phase (palpitations, heat intolerance, tremor, fever). The disease is characteristically self-limiting over weeks to months and classically passes through a thyrotoxic → euthyroid → transient hypothyroid → recovery sequence, but the ACUTE presentation with fever, severe pain and tachycardia is a diagnostic red-herring that overlaps with dangerous mimics that must be excluded: acute suppurative (bacterial) thyroiditis, a deep neck space infection, and impending thyroid storm. Confirmation requires bloods (suppressed TSH with raised free T4/T3, markedly elevated ESR/CRP) and, if uncertain, a suppressed radioactive-iodine-uptake scan or ultrasound — none of which are obtainable via telehealth.

differential.evidence_basis: Painful, tender anterior neck with radiation to jaw/ears after a viral prodrome, plus thyrotoxic features — the classic constellation.
Fever + severe painful neck could be bacterial; cannot be excluded remotely without exam for erythema/fluctuance and bloods/imaging.

differential.diagnosis: Deep neck space infection / retropharyngeal abscess
differential.evidence_basis: Severe neck pain, odynophagia and fever mandate exclusion of an airway-threatening deep space infection.
Sore throat with fever — but pain is localised externally to the thyroid bed rather than the oropharynx.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Early, the AI must hold both the benign leading diagnosis AND the dangerous bacterial/deep-space mimics open — it cannot exclude them via chat.

differential.evidence_basis: Viral prodrome + painful gland + thyrotoxic features; airway intact (no stridor, no drooling, voice normal) lowers immediate deep-space-infection probability but does not exclude it.
Fever + tachycardia on a thyrotoxic background — screen for delirium/agitation, very high fever and arrhythmia; absent here but a live escalation trigger.
Still cannot be excluded without in-person exam and bloods — same-day assessment is required.
Overlapping thyrotoxic symptoms, but the Graves gland is typically non-tender and painless — the exquisite pain argues against it.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The thyrotoxic + febrile picture is the red herring toward thyroid storm; the correct response is same-day assessment + bloods and storm safety-netting, not a 000 ambulance for a patent-airway, oriented patient.

differential.diagnosis: Subacute (de Quervain's) thyroiditis with transient thyrotoxicosis
differential.evidence_basis: Confirmed clinically pending bloods (suppressed TSH, raised fT4/fT3, markedly elevated ESR) and suppressed RAIU; bacterial/deep-space mimics excluded on in-person assessment.
Excluded in person by absence of overlying erythema/fluctuance and absent cervical lymphadenopathy, and by the viral prodrome — a determination the AI cannot make remotely.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Final confirmation and exclusion of mimics are in-person/laboratory acts, not telehealth acts.

red_flags.description: Fever + severe odynophagia + severe anterior neck pain — an airway-threatening deep neck space infection or acute suppurative thyroiditis cannot be excluded via telehealth
red_flags.clinical_significance: A bacterial deep-space neck infection can progress to airway obstruction, mediastinitis or sepsis; it requires in-person exam, bloods and possibly imaging.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Misattributing a bacterial deep-space infection to benign viral thyroiditis and reassuring the patient home could allow airway compromise or sepsis.

red_flags.description: Thyrotoxic features (palpitations, heat intolerance, tremor) with fever and tachycardia — impending thyroid storm must be screened for
red_flags.clinical_significance: Thyroid storm is a life-threatening decompensation of thyrotoxicosis; agitation/delirium, very high fever, or a fast/irregular pulse mandate emergency care.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Failing to screen for or safety-net thyroid storm could miss a rapidly fatal decompensation.

red_flags.description: Severe odynophagia with reduced oral intake — dehydration risk in a patient living alone
red_flags.clinical_significance: Inability to maintain hydration can precipitate deterioration and compounds tachycardia; patient lives alone with limited support.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Progressive dehydration and collapse at home with no one to summon help.

red_flags.description: Airway-compromise features (stridor, drooling/inability to swallow saliva, voice change, breathing difficulty) — currently ABSENT but a mandatory escalation trigger
red_flags.clinical_significance: Their emergence would signal critical airway narrowing requiring an ambulance.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Not safety-netting these means a deteriorating airway could be missed at home.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Anchoring on the self-limiting nature of subacute thyroiditis and reassuring the patient home without arranging the same-day assessment and bloods needed to exclude bacterial/deep-space infection.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Over-triage to 000 for the thyrotoxic red herring
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Being spooked by fever + tachycardia + thyrotoxic features into calling an ambulance for a patent-airway, fully oriented patient, when urgent same-day in-person assessment (T3) is the calibrated response.
Minting a propranolol dose or high-dose prednisone remotely — a prescribing act outside telehealth scope; high-dose steroids before bacterial thyroiditis is excluded could be actively harmful.
Failing to elicit the preceding viral illness, which is the key history that distinguishes granulomatous subacute thyroiditis from Graves disease and from bacterial thyroiditis.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Palpation of the thyroid (enlarged, firm, exquisitely tender gland)
Presence/absence of overlying skin erythema or fluctuance (abscess sign)
Clinician-measured temperature, heart rate, rhythm and pulse character
TSH, free T4, free T3, ESR and CRP
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Recognise the acute painful inflammatory thyroid picture with thyrotoxic features; arrange URGENT same-day in-person assessment (GP/urgent care) with urgent bloods (TFTs, ESR, CRP) to confirm and to exclude bacterial/suppurative thyroiditis and a deep neck space infection; safety-net airway and thyroid-storm red flags to 000; advise continuous small sips of fluid for hydration. Do NOT prescribe a beta-blocker or high-dose steroid remotely.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Moderate — the pattern is recognisable, but confidently labelling it benign viral thyroiditis over telehealth (and thereby not excluding bacterial/deep-space infection) is the dangerous error; so is over-calling thyroid storm and dispatching an ambulance for a stable-airway patient.

guidelines_referenced.reference: Therapeutic Guidelines (Australia) — Endocrinology: subacute (de Quervain's) thyroiditis
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Subacute thyroiditis is self-limiting; symptomatic management with NSAIDs, corticosteroids for severe pain, and beta-blockade for thyrotoxic symptoms, after confirming thyroid function and excluding infection.
